scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a regulated-inventory availability summary grounded in package tags, hold posture, vendor timing, and authoritative systems of record.
Missing jurisdiction, registrant, and system prerequisites were treated as blockers instead of being guessed.
The shortage was escalated with traceable evidence and without unsupported substitute availability.
Vendor delay was converted into a bounded action note with dependency context rather than an ungrounded promise of recovery.
The lane preserved release and ordering controls and refused to bypass regulated approvals.
Substitution pressure was routed to licensed or policy owners instead of being absorbed as routine inventory work.
Incomplete manifest, count, and traceability evidence correctly blocked movement recommendations.
The lane rejected adjacent specialist work involving formal regulatory reporting or destruction authority.
Low-confidence handling named the missing industry facts and avoided cross-regime improvisation.
The lane explicitly refused to collapse regulated cannabis or controlled-substance handling into generic warehouse behavior.
Conflicting public and tenant controls were escalated for human resolution rather than synthesized into a confident answer.
Potential diversion or significant-loss signals were escalated with evidence packaging while preserving formal reporting ownership.
Tenant terminology such as CCTT, CTS, CSOS, ARCOS, and Form 106 was normalized into authoritative records and assumptions.

notes: All 13 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to regulated inventory traceability, replenishment, shortage handling, and reporting boundaries.
Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all supply-procurement-and-availability abilities, and the required cross-industry ability.
